cards.front: What is the primary function of the rough endoplasmic reticulum?
cards.back: It is studded with ribosomes and synthesizes and modifies proteins destined for secretion, the plasma membrane, or the endomembrane system.

cards.front: Describe the fluid mosaic model of the plasma membrane.
cards.back: A phospholipid bilayer with embedded and peripheral proteins, cholesterol, and carbohydrates whose components move laterally, giving the membrane fluidity and selective permeability.

cards.front: Which organelle is the main site of aerobic ATP production?
cards.back: The mitochondrion, which carries out the citric acid cycle and oxidative phosphorylation to generate most cellular ATP.

cards.front: What structural features distinguish eukaryotic from prokaryotic cells?
cards.back: Eukaryotic cells have a membrane-bound nucleus and membrane-bound organelles, while prokaryotic cells lack both and keep their DNA in a nucleoid region.

cards.front: What is the role of the Golgi apparatus?
cards.back: It modifies, sorts, and packages proteins and lipids received from the ER into vesicles for secretion or delivery to other organelles.

cards.front: How do lysosomes contribute to cell function?
cards.back: They contain hydrolytic enzymes active at acidic pH that digest macromolecules, worn-out organelles, and material taken in by the cell.

questions.stem: A eukaryotic cell that secretes large quantities of protein hormone would be expected to contain an especially abundant amount of which organelle?
questions.explanation: Ribosomes on the rough ER synthesize proteins that enter the endomembrane system for secretion, so secretory cells are rich in rough ER.

questions.stem: What is the primary role of cholesterol within a mammalian plasma membrane?
options: It forms hydrophilic channels for ions
It supplies ATP for active transport
It buffers membrane fluidity across a range of temperatures
It anchors the cell to the extracellular matrix
questions.explanation: Cholesterol inserts among phospholipids and stabilizes fluidity, keeping the membrane from becoming too rigid when cold or too fluid when warm.

questions.stem: A toxin that blocks the electron transport chain would most directly impair the function of which organelle?
questions.explanation: The electron transport chain resides in the inner mitochondrial membrane, so blocking it halts oxidative phosphorylation and mitochondrial ATP production.

questions.stem: Which feature is found in eukaryotic cells but absent in prokaryotic cells?
questions.explanation: Only eukaryotic cells enclose their DNA in a membrane-bound nucleus; the plasma membrane, ribosomes, and cytoplasm are common to both.

cards.front: What is a bacteriophage?
cards.back: A virus that infects bacteria, typically attaching to the cell and injecting its nucleic acid while the protein capsid remains outside.

cards.front: How do the lytic and lysogenic cycles of a phage differ?
cards.back: In the lytic cycle the phage replicates and lyses the host quickly; in the lysogenic cycle the phage genome integrates as a prophage and replicates passively with the host.

cards.front: What are the three mechanisms of horizontal gene transfer in bacteria?
cards.back: Transformation (uptake of free DNA from the environment), transduction (transfer of DNA via a bacteriophage), and conjugation (transfer via a pilus or plasmid).

cards.front: How does the Gram stain distinguish bacteria?
cards.back: Gram-positive cells have a thick peptidoglycan wall that retains crystal violet and appear purple; Gram-negative cells have a thin wall plus an outer membrane and stain pink with safranin.

cards.front: What is a retrovirus?
cards.back: An RNA virus that uses reverse transcriptase to make DNA from its RNA genome, which then integrates into the host chromosome; HIV is a well-known example.

cards.front: How do bacteria typically reproduce?
cards.back: By binary fission, an asexual process in which the cell replicates its DNA and divides into two genetically identical daughter cells without a mitotic spindle.

questions.stem: A bacterium gains an antibiotic-resistance gene after direct cell-to-cell contact through a pilus that transfers a plasmid. Which process is described?
questions.explanation: Conjugation transfers genetic material, often a plasmid, from one bacterium to another through a pilus-mediated contact.

questions.stem: During the lysogenic cycle of a bacteriophage, the viral genome most characteristically:
options: Integrates into the host chromosome as a prophage
Is packaged into new virions and released
Is degraded by host restriction enzymes
questions.explanation: In lysogeny the phage DNA integrates into the host genome as a prophage and is replicated along with the host DNA rather than causing immediate lysis.

questions.stem: Reverse transcriptase, the enzyme used by retroviruses, catalyzes the synthesis of:
questions.explanation: Reverse transcriptase makes complementary DNA from the viral RNA genome, reversing the usual DNA-to-RNA flow of information.

questions.stem: A Gram-negative bacterium differs from a Gram-positive bacterium primarily because it possesses:
questions.explanation: Gram-negative bacteria have a thin peptidoglycan layer surrounded by an outer membrane rich in lipopolysaccharide, which is why they do not retain crystal violet.

cards.front: What are the three subphases of interphase?
cards.back: G1 (cell growth and normal function), S (DNA replication), and G2 (further growth and preparation for mitosis).

cards.front: What occurs during metaphase of mitosis?
cards.back: The duplicated chromosomes align single file along the metaphase plate at the cell equator, each attached to spindle fibers at its kinetochore.

cards.front: What does the G1/S checkpoint monitor?
cards.back: It checks cell size, nutrient and energy reserves, growth signals, and DNA integrity before the cell commits to DNA replication.

cards.front: How do totipotent and pluripotent stem cells differ?
cards.back: Totipotent cells can give rise to all cell types including extraembryonic (placental) tissue, whereas pluripotent cells can form all body cell types but not extraembryonic tissue.

cards.front: What is cellular differentiation?
cards.back: The process by which a less specialized cell becomes a specialized cell type through selective gene expression, without changing its DNA sequence.

cards.front: How does mitosis differ from meiosis in outcome?
cards.back: Mitosis produces two genetically identical diploid cells, while meiosis produces four genetically distinct haploid cells for sexual reproduction.

questions.stem: DNA is replicated during which phase of the cell cycle?
questions.explanation: DNA synthesis occurs during the S (synthesis) phase of interphase, producing identical sister chromatids for each chromosome.

questions.stem: Which event defines anaphase of mitosis?
options: Chromosomes align at the metaphase plate
Sister chromatids separate and move to opposite poles
questions.explanation: During anaphase the cohesion between sister chromatids breaks and the chromatids are pulled toward opposite spindle poles.

questions.stem: A stem cell that can differentiate into any cell type of the body but cannot form extraembryonic tissue such as placenta is best described as:
questions.explanation: Pluripotent cells can generate all germ-layer cell types of the body but lack the ability to form extraembryonic tissues, unlike totipotent cells.

questions.stem: What is the primary purpose of the cell cycle checkpoints?
options: To generate genetic variation among cells
To trigger apoptosis in every dividing cell
To ensure conditions and DNA integrity are correct before advancing
questions.explanation: Checkpoints verify that requirements such as intact DNA and proper chromosome attachment are met before the cell proceeds, preventing propagation of errors.

cards.front: What maintains the resting membrane potential of a neuron?
cards.back: The Na+/K+ ATPase pumps Na+ out and K+ in, and with the membrane most permeable to K+ the interior settles near -70 mV.
cards.source: OpenStax Anatomy and Physiology 2e - Nervous tissue

cards.front: What triggers neurotransmitter release at a chemical synapse?
cards.back: Arrival of the action potential opens voltage-gated Ca2+ channels; Ca2+ influx causes synaptic vesicles to fuse with the membrane and release neurotransmitter.

cards.front: Contrast the sympathetic and parasympathetic divisions of the autonomic nervous system.
cards.back: The sympathetic division drives fight-or-flight responses such as increased heart rate, while the parasympathetic division promotes rest-and-digest activity.
cards.source: OpenStax Anatomy and Physiology 2e - Autonomic nervous system

cards.front: How do steroid hormones act on target cells?
cards.back: Being lipid-soluble, they diffuse across the plasma membrane and bind intracellular receptors that act as transcription factors to alter gene expression.
cards.source: OpenStax Anatomy and Physiology 2e - Endocrine system

cards.front: What is the role of the hypothalamus in the endocrine system?
cards.back: It links the nervous and endocrine systems, secreting releasing and inhibiting hormones that regulate the anterior pituitary and producing ADH and oxytocin.
cards.source: OpenStax Anatomy and Physiology 2e - Endocrine system

cards.front: What is saltatory conduction?
cards.back: The rapid propagation of an action potential that appears to jump between the nodes of Ranvier along a myelinated axon, increasing conduction speed.
cards.source: OpenStax Anatomy and Physiology 2e - Nervous tissue

questions.stem: The resting membrane potential of a typical neuron is closest to which value?
questions.explanation: A typical neuron rests near -70 mV because the membrane is most permeable to K+ and the Na+/K+ ATPase maintains the ion gradients.
questions.source: OpenStax Anatomy and Physiology 2e - Nervous tissue

questions.stem: The rapid depolarizing (rising) phase of a neuronal action potential is produced mainly by:
options: K+ efflux out of the cell
Ca2+ efflux out of the cell
questions.explanation: Voltage-gated Na+ channels open at threshold, and the resulting Na+ influx drives the membrane toward positive potentials during depolarization.
questions.source: OpenStax Anatomy and Physiology 2e - Nervous tissue

questions.stem: Steroid hormones typically exert their effects by binding receptors located:
options: Inside the cell, in the cytoplasm or nucleus
Only on the extracellular membrane surface
On the outer face of the Golgi apparatus
questions.explanation: Because steroid hormones are lipid-soluble, they cross the membrane and bind intracellular receptors that regulate transcription.
questions.source: OpenStax Anatomy and Physiology 2e - Endocrine system

questions.stem: Activation of the sympathetic nervous system would most likely produce which response?
questions.explanation: The sympathetic fight-or-flight response raises heart rate; increased salivation, pupil constriction, and digestive stimulation are parasympathetic effects.
questions.source: OpenStax Anatomy and Physiology 2e - Autonomic nervous system

cards.front: Trace the path of blood through the heart beginning at the venae cavae.
cards.back: Venae cavae to right atrium to right ventricle to pulmonary arteries to lungs to pulmonary veins to left atrium to left ventricle to aorta.
cards.source: OpenStax Anatomy and Physiology 2e - Cardiovascular system

cards.front: Where does most nutrient absorption occur in the digestive tract?
cards.back: In the small intestine, whose villi and microvilli enormously increase surface area for absorbing digested nutrients.
cards.source: OpenStax Anatomy and Physiology 2e - Digestive system

cards.front: What is the functional unit of the kidney and what does it do?
cards.back: The nephron, which forms urine through filtration of blood at the glomerulus followed by tubular reabsorption and secretion.
cards.source: OpenStax Anatomy and Physiology 2e - Urinary system

cards.front: How is oxygen transported in the blood?
cards.back: Most oxygen binds reversibly to hemoglobin in red blood cells, with only a small fraction dissolved directly in the plasma.
cards.source: OpenStax Anatomy and Physiology 2e - Respiratory system

cards.front: What drives inhalation during quiet breathing?
cards.back: Contraction of the diaphragm and external intercostals expands the thoracic cavity, lowering intrapulmonary pressure so air flows into the lungs.
cards.source: OpenStax Anatomy and Physiology 2e - Respiratory system

cards.front: How is most carbon dioxide carried in the blood?
cards.back: Mainly as bicarbonate ions formed in red blood cells by carbonic anhydrase, with smaller amounts bound to hemoglobin and dissolved in plasma.
cards.source: OpenStax Anatomy and Physiology 2e - Respiratory system

questions.stem: Oxygenated blood returns from the lungs to the heart through the:
questions.explanation: The pulmonary veins carry freshly oxygenated blood from the lungs into the left atrium; the pulmonary artery instead carries deoxygenated blood to the lungs.
questions.source: OpenStax Anatomy and Physiology 2e - Cardiovascular system

questions.stem: In what form is the majority of carbon dioxide transported in the blood?
options: Dissolved as CO2 gas in plasma
questions.explanation: About two-thirds of CO2 is carried as bicarbonate, generated when carbonic anhydrase in red blood cells converts CO2 and water to carbonic acid that dissociates.
questions.source: OpenStax Anatomy and Physiology 2e - Respiratory system

questions.stem: The functional filtering unit of the kidney is the:
questions.explanation: The nephron filters blood and processes the filtrate into urine; alveoli, villi, and sarcomeres are units of the lung, intestine, and muscle respectively.
questions.source: OpenStax Anatomy and Physiology 2e - Urinary system

questions.stem: During inhalation at rest, the diaphragm:
questions.explanation: Contraction flattens the diaphragm downward, increasing thoracic volume and lowering pressure so air enters the lungs.
questions.source: OpenStax Anatomy and Physiology 2e - Respiratory system
